title: Yanto Chandra 蔡振榮
text:
I am a professor at the City University of Hong Kong. My research lies at the intersection of innovation and strategy in the public and social sectors. I use data science, computational social science, and experimental designs to make policies and societies better and happier. I’m also applying textual data to make reproducible inference. Ocassionally, I do conceptual work for breaking new ground. Theory is important but I am not theory-fetish. My new mission: go beyond journal publications. Turn research into real-world artefacts: tools, apps, and repositories.
I have published in reputable journals in public administration and policy, including Public Administration Review, Public Management Review, Public Administration, Policy Studies Journal , and also in management and organizations such as  Journal of Management Studies, Academy of Management Review, Journal of Business Venturing, Journal of International Business Studies. 
I work with R/RStudio, Python, LaTeX, Quarto, Markdown, GitHub, Netlify, and I’m always looking for new tools to make my research better.

  Email    LinkedIn    GitHub    Google Scholar

title: GPT for social scientists?
text:
In early 2023, I and my collaborator have worked really hard to design a methodological framework using GPT for scale development and synthetic data generation to measure, predict, retrodict, and calibrate innovation outcomes using real-world and synthetic data and a human-in-the-loop process in competitive settings, where many compete but few will be winners.
After multiple rounds with super critical reviewers, this work was published finally (still early view) in Public Administration Review, an excellent journal in public administration.   Article

title: The end of social enterprise?
text:
Social enterprise has been popularly seen as a form of business. While this is not wrong, it is a very narrow view of what it really is. Social enterprise is not just a business, it is a tool to enact policy change by working outside, inside and with the government. In this way, it can unleash more social impact and change the world for the better.
Our paper, with data collected for nearly a decade on nine policy changing social enterprises in the Global Soutn recently won an award – The Jenei Award 2025 – awarded at the recent IRSPM 2026 conference in Perth, Australia. It was published in Public Management Review   Published

title: Dancing with Quarto
text: I’ve been diving into Quarto lately, and honestly, the thing that blows me away is how ridiculously versatile 😄😄 it is. Basically, it’s a tool for publishing stuff—like reports, blogs, even entire books—but the magic is that once written it can magically turn into a PDF, a website, a Word doc, or even a cool beamer-style presentation. The other really cool part is: you can ✅toss in some codes from Python or R in a document, and Quarto handles all the messy behind-the-scenes work. So whether I need to send a quick ✅memo, whip up a blog ✅or churn a manuscript and use the exact same workflow 🔄, which feels like cheating 🔓🔓 but in the best way possible.

title: Visualizing penguins
text:
Here is what I did with the palmer penguins dataset in Python. I created a beautiful forest plot to visualize the standardized differences (Cohen’s d) between the three penguin species across four morphological metrics: bill length, bill depth, flipper length, and body mass. Each point represents the effect size for a specific comparison between two species, with confidence intervals shown as horizontal lines. The plot is designed to be visually appealing and informative, making it easy to interpret the differences between the species.
Hint: there were too many bugs and it took forever to get the codes right.
Original dataset is here: palmerpenguins on GitHub
